text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY SURVEILLANCE FOLLOW-UP NOTE

Patient: Renata Okonkwo
Age: 40-41 years
Visit Type: Routine Oncology Surveillance — 18-Month Post-Surgical Follow-Up
ECOG Performance Status: 0-1

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Okonkwo presents for her 18-month post-surgical oncology surveillance visit. She is here for routine clinical assessment, review of recent imaging, and ongoing survivorship planning. She has no new symptoms of concern at today's visit.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Okonkwo is a 40-to-41-year-old Nigerian-American woman with a history of Stage IIB (ypT1b ypN1 M0 after neoadjuvant therapy) triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast, diagnosed approximately 18 months ago. She is a confirmed BRCA1 germline pathogenic variant carrier (c.5074del, p.Cys1692ValfsTer17), which significantly shaped her surgical and surveillance planning. She has completed all intended curative-intent systemic therapy and surgery.

For context, her treatment course is as follows: She received neoadjuvant pembro (Keytruda) 200 mg IV every 3 weeks combined with dose-dense doxo/cyclo (ddAC) every 2 weeks for 4 cycles (KEYNOTE-522 regimen), followed by weekly pacli (Taxol) for 12 weeks plus pembro every 3 weeks. She then underwent bilateral nipple-sparing mastectomy — left modified radical mastectomy with sentinel lymph node biopsy and axillary lymph node dissection, plus right prophylactic mastectomy — with immediate tissue expander placement. Surgical pathology confirmed non-pathologic complete response (non-pCR): ypT1b ypN1 M0 with RCB class II (intermediate residual disease burden). She subsequently received adjuvant cape (Xeloda) 1000 mg/m2 BID (dose-reduced to 750 mg/m2 after Grade 2-3 hand-foot syndrome) for 8 cycles, combined with Keytruda maintenance to complete a total of 17 doses (approximately one year) across both neoadjuvant and adjuvant phases. Her final dose of pembro was complicated by Grade 2 immune-mediated pneumonitis, which resolved fully with prednisone. She also underwent risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (RRSO) approximately 8 months ago, with pathology showing no malignancy.

At today's visit, she reports feeling well overall. She has returned to work full time as a high school biology teacher and notes that her energy levels have substantially recovered. She describes persistent but mild peripheral neuropathy — primarily trace tingling at the fingertips bilaterally — which she characterizes as intermittent and non-interfering with daily activities or professional duties. She has been managing surgical menopause symptoms (hot flashes, night sweats, mood fluctuations, and vaginal dryness) since her RRSO. Hot flashes have improved significantly on venlafaxine (Effexor) 75 mg daily, which she rates as effective. She continues to use non-hormonal vaginal moisturizers and lubricants, and reports tolerating intercourse without significant difficulty. She denies chest pain, dyspnea, cough, or any respiratory symptoms since resolution of her pneumonitis. She denies new lumps, skin changes over the chest wall or mastectomy sites, axillary swelling, bone pain, persistent headaches, or neurological changes. She reports no GI symptoms, abdominal pain, or jaundice. She was evaluated by the breast MRI surveillance protocol at the 12-month mark, which she recalls as unremarkable.

Emotionally, she acknowledges ongoing adjustment to her diagnosis, BRCA1 carrier status, and early surgical menopause. She remains engaged with the oncology social worker and has recently joined a young survivors support group, which she finds meaningful. She notes her mother and maternal aunt's histories weigh on her, and she is actively facilitating genetic testing awareness for her family members, including preparing materials for her two children who will be eligible for testing in the future. She has met again with genetic counseling to discuss cascade testing strategy.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

- Constitutional: Fatigue improved significantly compared to prior visits; now rates energy at approximately 8/10. No fevers, chills, or unintentional weight loss.
- HEENT: No headaches, visual changes, or new lymphadenopathy in neck or supraclavicular region.
- Pulmonary: No dyspnea at rest or with routine exertion. No cough, hemoptysis, or pleuritic chest pain. Pneumonitis fully resolved.
- Cardiovascular: No palpitations (palpitations previously resolved with discontinuation of pembro after thyrotoxic phase of immune thyroiditis). No edema.
- GI: Mild residual constipation, which she attributes to levothyroxine-level fluctuation; no diarrhea, no blood in stool, no abdominal pain.
- Musculoskeletal: Mild arthralgia in the small joints of the hands and feet, non-progressive; residual from pacli and/or menopause-related joint symptoms. Denies new bone pain at rest or at night.
- Neurological: Peripheral neuropathy, trace-to-Grade 1, bilateral fingertips only. No motor deficits. No new central symptoms.
- Dermatologic: Bilateral mastectomy scars well healed. Stage 2 reconstruction with silicone implants in place bilaterally; she is satisfied with cosmetic outcome. No skin erythema, ulceration, or peau d'orange changes over chest wall.
- Genitourinary/Reproductive: Surgical menopause symptoms as described. Vaginal dryness managed with local moisturizers.
- Endocrine: Hypothyroidism stable on levothyroxine. No recurrent thyrotoxic symptoms.
- Psychiatric/Psychosocial: Anxiety managed with support group and counseling. Denies depression requiring pharmacotherapy at this time.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 118/74 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16, temperature 98.4°F, SpO2 98% on room air, weight 67 kg (baseline 68 kg at start of chemotherapy; essentially stable).

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. Pleasant and engaged in conversation. Dressed professionally. Appears her stated age.

Head, Eyes, Ears, Nose, Throat: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Pupils equal and reactive. Conjunctivae clear, anicteric sclerae. Oropharynx without mucositis or lesions. No thyroid enlargement or tenderness on palpation. No cervical, supraclavicular, or infraclavicular lymphadenopathy.

Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles, wheezes, or rhonchi. Normal respiratory effort. Good air entry throughout.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

Breasts and Chest Wall: Bilateral nipple-sparing mastectomy sites with well-healed transverse scars. Silicone implants bilaterally — soft, appropriately positioned, no capsular contracture appreciated on gross exam. No palpable masses, chest wall nodules, or skin thickening. No peau d'orange. Nipples intact and without discharge. Left axilla: no palpable adenopathy. Right axilla: no palpable adenopathy. No supraclavicular adenopathy.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatomegaly or splenomegaly. No palpable masses. Well-healed laparoscopic port sites from prior RRSO.

Extremities: No upper or lower extremity edema. No lymphedema of the left upper extremity (at-risk limb post-axillary dissection). Sensation to light touch mildly reduced at the fingertips bilaterally; intact proximally. No motor deficits. Mild tenderness over the metatarsophalangeal joints bilaterally — consistent with known residual post-pacli arthralgia.

Neurological: Alert and oriented x4. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Strength 5/5 in all major muscle groups. Gait normal.

---

LAB RESULTS

CBC: WBC 6.1 x10^3/uL, Hgb 12.7 g/dL, Plt 234 x10^3/uL. ANC 3,800/uL. No cytopenias.
Comprehensive Metabolic Panel: Sodium 139, Potassium 4.0, Creatinine 0.74 mg/dL, BUN 11, ALT 24 U/L, AST 22 U/L, Alk Phos 68 U/L, Total Bilirubin 0.5 mg/dL, Albumin 4.1 g/dL, Glucose 89 mg/dL. All within normal limits. No hepatotoxicity.
TSH: 2.9 mIU/L (within normal range on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily — stable).
Note: CA 27.29 and CA 15-3 tumor markers are not ordered per ASCO guidelines, as serial tumor marker testing is not recommended for TNBC surveillance and has not been shown to improve outcomes in this population.

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Annual chest wall/skin surveillance breast MRI (bilateral reconstructed breasts, gadolinium-enhanced) — performed at the 12-month post-surgical timepoint per high-risk surveillance protocol: No evidence of locoregional recurrence. No abnormal enhancement in the chest wall, skin, or residual breast tissue. Bilateral silicone implants intact without capsular abnormality. No new axillary lymphadenopathy.

Prior surveillance CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed at the 6-month post-surgery mark: No evidence of locoregional or distant recurrence. No pulmonary nodules. Bilateral implants in place. No hepatic lesions. Post-surgical changes from RRSO noted. All lymph node stations unremarkable.

At today's visit, I reviewed the most recent MRI report in detail. I am not planning to obtain repeat CT imaging at this visit given the 12-month MRI was clean and there are no clinical findings suggesting new metastatic disease. Will defer next CT to the standard surveillance interval unless symptoms emerge.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Triple-Negative Breast Cancer, Stage IIB, BRCA1-Associated — No Evidence of Disease (NED) at 18 Months Post-Surgery.
   Ms. Okonkwo has now completed approximately 18 months of follow-up from her bilateral mastectomy with no clinical or radiographic evidence of recurrence. She completed the full KEYNOTE-522 regimen (neoadjuvant pembro + ddAC + Taxol) and adjuvant Xeloda (8 cycles) plus pembro maintenance (17 total doses). Despite a non-pCR (RCB class II, ypT1b ypN1 M0) — which carries a higher recurrence risk than pCR, particularly in the first 3-5 years — she has remained disease-free to date. Her BRCA1 carrier status remains an ongoing consideration both for recurrence risk in the chest wall/skin and for the previously addressed ovarian cancer risk (now mitigated by completed RRSO). I had an extensive discussion with her today about her overall prognosis, acknowledging both the favorable trajectory thus far and the importance of maintaining close surveillance.
   - Continue surveillance visits every 3-4 months through year 3, then every 6 months through year 5, then annually.
   - Next surveillance CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to be performed at 24 months post-surgery (approximately 6 months from today's visit) unless new symptoms arise.
   - Annual breast MRI of chest wall/bilateral reconstruction sites to continue per high-risk protocol, given BRCA1 carrier status and mastectomy (residual chest wall/skin at risk).
   - No role for PET-CT in routine surveillance.

2. BRCA1 Germline Pathogenic Variant — Ongoing Survivorship and Family Implications.
   She has completed RRSO with benign pathology and is now surgical menopausal. Uterus retained per patient preference. No further RRSO-related oncologic concerns. She is counseled that given BRCA1, risk of primary peritoneal carcinoma remains (small but real even post-RRSO), and she should report any new pelvic or abdominal symptoms. She understands this.
   - Cascade genetic testing for family members (sister, brother, and her children at appropriate age) encouraged and facilitated.
   - Continued co-management with gynecologic oncology for annual pelvic exams and symptom surveillance.
   - Regarding PARP inhibitor use: Lynparza (Lynparza) was discussed at the time of adjuvant planning and deferred in favor of Xeloda + pembro (per OlympiA data, ola is approved as adjuvant therapy for BRCA-mutated, HER2-negative high-risk early BC after chemotherapy). Patient and I discussed today that she has already completed her adjuvant cape and pembro, and the window for adjuvant ola per the OlympiA trial design has effectively closed in the conventional sense. However, she understands that BRCA1 carrier status remains highly actionable in the metastatic setting should recurrence ever occur, and we would strongly consider a PARP inhibitor at that point.

3. Immune-Related Hypothyroidism — Stable.
   Levothyroxine 75 mcg daily continued without dose change. TSH 2.9 mIU/L — excellent control. No symptoms of hypo- or hyperthyroidism. Will continue annual TSH monitoring. Endocrinology does not require further follow-up at this time; management transitioned to PCP and oncology.

4. Peripheral Neuropathy — Improving, Trace-Grade 1.
   Residual bilateral fingertip neuropathy following dose-dense AC and pacli regimen (pacli dose had been reduced 20% for Grade 2 neuropathy during treatment). Significant improvement since end of treatment — symptoms now minimal. No functional limitations. I do not anticipate further significant improvement but do not expect worsening either. No pharmacologic treatment needed. She is counseled on fall precautions and foot care. Referral for occupational therapy declined by patient at this time (symptoms are mild).

5. Surgical Menopause — Managed Non-Hormonally.
   Since RRSO approximately 8 months ago, she has been in surgical menopause. She declined systemic hormone replacement therapy given breast cancer history (TNBC), and I support this decision. Venlafaxine 75 mg daily has been effective for vasomotor symptoms (hot flashes now rated as mild, down from moderate at initiation). She is using non-estrogen vaginal moisturizer and lubricants for genitourinary symptoms. Sleep hygiene counseling reviewed. Bone health: Given early surgical menopause and BRCA1 status, will obtain a baseline DEXA scan within the next 6 months to assess for premature bone density loss. If osteopenia identified, bisphosphonate therapy or RANK-L inhibitor (denosumab) will be considered. Calcium 1,200 mg and vitamin D 1,000 IU daily recommended and patient is already taking these.

6. Psychosocial Support and Quality of Life.
   Ms. Okonkwo continues to engage meaningfully with the oncology social worker and a young survivors group. She has returned to full-time employment and reports high-quality interpersonal relationships and life engagement. Emotionally, she is navigating survivorship anxiety with insight and appropriate coping strategies. No formal psychiatric referral needed at this time. Integrative oncology referral previously placed for acupuncture (for hot flashes and neuropathy) — patient reports she has attended two sessions and found mild benefit. Will continue to support holistic care strategies. Fertility: She had previously banked embryos prior to chemotherapy — those embryos remain available. She has not yet decided on future family planning steps, and she understands the complexities given her BRCA1 status and surgical menopause (ovum donation or gestational surrogacy would be necessary). No urgent action needed; will revisit when she raises this.

7. Survivorship Care Plan.
   A comprehensive survivorship care plan is on file and has been updated today. Summary of late effects monitoring: annual thyroid function, cardiac monitoring (EF was 59% post-doxo — plan for echocardiogram at 2 years post-completion of Adriamycin, currently approximately 12-14 months from last echo), DEXA scan for bone health, and annual breast MRI as above. Primary care provider has been sent an updated summary letter.

Return to clinic in approximately 3-4 months for next surveillance visit. Sooner if new symptoms arise — specifically instructed to call if she notes: new chest wall nodule, skin changes over mastectomy sites, new axillary or cervical lymph node, persistent back or hip pain, new neurological symptoms, or respiratory symptoms.

Total visit time: 45 minutes. Complexity: High (multiple active survivorship issues, BRCA1 management, psychosocial complexity, post-immunotherapy monitoring).

Dictated and finalized by: Attending Medical Oncologist

---

MEDICATIONS (Current)
1. Levothyroxine 75 mcg orally daily (immune thyroiditis — hypothyroid phase, stable)
2. Venlafaxine (Effexor XR) 75 mg orally daily (surgical menopause vasomotor symptoms)
3. Calcium carbonate 600 mg with vitamin D orally twice daily
4. Non-estrogen vaginal moisturizer (Replens or equivalent) 3x weekly PRN
5. Ibuprofen 400-600 mg orally PRN arthralgia (used sparingly)

ALLERGIES: NKDA

COPIES: Primary Care Physician, Genetic Counselor, Gynecologic Oncology
